channel.title: Posts on Musings on the modern world

item.title: Press Reset - Review
item.description:
Press Reset by video game journalist Jason Schreier details how unstable development is with its always looming layoffs, office moves across the country and project cancellations.

The book starts in the late 80s and early 90s with famous video game designer Warren Specter, detailing his move across various publishers, trying to make games he is passionate about but which never quite hit the nerve of a mass audience. From there on, the book moves forward through the decades, covering the experiences of dozens of industry figures. The interesting thing with the book’s storytelling is that you start to realise how small the industry is. Although Schreier mostly focuses on individual projects in each chapter, employee and company names repeat throughout and start to ring a bell based on earlier chapters. It becomes obvious how often employees have to jump from company to company, often across the entire American continent, uprooting their lives only for having to be laid off yet again and having to move another time.

Reading this book makes you realise how difficult it must be to create a game: Usually, multiple studios are working on the same title, often spread across different cities or even continents. Many of these games don’t even see the light of day because suddenly the publisher might decide that the project is not good enough. And if a game comes out, it is not always a given that this project is a success: not every title can be an Uncharted - or whatever your favourite game is. In fact, many of the titles mentioned in the book were unknown to me or were reported by Schreier to be a failure in some way or form. This stands completely at odds with the many sacrifices creators make as we learn throughout the book. Workers in the industry give their all, both in terms of life time and life quality, just to fulfil their dream of making games.

If you are interested too then you might start reading this article on the outlet Polygon which details the numerous ways Bioshock Infinite was a sheer catastrophe to work on, despite the overall fidelity of the final product. This article is an excerpt from Schreier’s Press Reset and I found it so fascinating that I just had to get the entire book. And I don’t regret it, I haven’t read a book which this much vigor in years. Highly recommended.

item.title: Mr. Robot - Series Review
item.description:
This post is divided into two sections: Section 1 contains general notes, also suitable for people who haven’t seen the show yet. Section 2 discusses the show’s major twists and should only be read by people who have seen the show.

Partition A:\General_opinion

I finally did it, I finished Mr. Robot! I don’t have a lot of focus for watching TV lately, which is why it took me slightly more than a year to finish the total of 45 episodes. And oh boy, what a ride it was!

Mr. Robot tells the story of Elliot, a brilliant hacker who tries to overthrow the mighty tech giant “E Corp” which controls the world through their products and finanical offerings. What starts as a dangerous but survivable activity turns over time into a life-threatening and crippling fight against also other organisations where it is hard to know who is friend and who is foe.

I don’t want to discuss the plot too strongly as I think it is something everyone should experience for themselves. But I can freely speak about Mr. Robot’s writing and direction. The show’s themes are always strongly divided into two: The show explores the bleak side of technological America and psychological themes relating to its characters. Over the course of the series, it feels like the tech theme takes a step back and the psychological aspects become more and more important. This was not a problem to me, but I can understand that other viewers may find it irritating, especially since the entire hacking and tech aspect has been so interesting. As a programmer myself I can attest that the hacking is usually (!) depicted quite realistically: Elliot uses real-life terminals and Linux systems, we see things like SSH keys and 2FA codes, password manglers… it is really satisfying to watch Elliot get his way with his targets. This makes one want to start learning how to hack themselves!

I enjoyed the show’s second main theme, psychology, even more. The show’s creator Sam Esmail (who nearly wrote and directed every single episode) often takes a very imaginative approach in depicting how characters feel, especially the main character: There are a lot of dream sequences, confusing montages, inter-linking dialogue and references to past events through camera angles, clothing and character positions. The dialogue is usually extremely impressive in the way that the characters don’t use too many lines, but every line is filled with meaning. Even more meaning is being evoked with the way things are directed, i.e. how characters are positioned, the speed they speak with, the music, the lighting… it is truly a directorial masterpiece. I am stunned that Esmail wrote and directed so many episodes, each of which is of the highest fidelity and quality, able to compete with major movies.

Partition B:\Major_plot_points_and_finale

Warning - This section contains spoilers of the entire show! Only read on if you already have seen it or if you are not interested in watching it.

Of course, it is the finale I would like to discuss more deeply. As a fan of how Twin Peaks Season 2 ended, Mr. Robot left me quite impressed because I felt that the show followed a similar approach: it traded the eery and uneasy feelings harboured throughout its entire run for full-blown madness and dreamscapes. I highly enjoyed this - but also can’t help feeling slapped in the face! Whenever a narrative plays with notions of reality and employs the twist that everything which was experienced previously was not real, viewers can’t help to feel cheated because they invested so much time and emotion into the story. Nobody likes it if you take that away from them!

Of course, it is never fully stated whether all the previous season’s events are imagined, but this was at least the impression I had on viewing. Let’s recap: After hacking the Deus Group, Elliot heads for the nuclear reactor to destroy Whiterose’s machine. He can simply wander into the plant without any difficulty and all he has to do is find a switched-on computer and plug in a USB stick - in this sequence, he doesn’t even type any words on the keyboard. Then he is being taken captive and has a conversation with Whiterose about society where she kills herself and starts a nuclear meltdown which Elliot can stop by solving a video game. Nevertheless, some sort of explosion happens. This explosion starts a dream sequence with a fantasy version of Elliot’s life. At the end of this dream sequence, he talks to his therapist Gloria and she reveals that Elliot is only some other split personality from the “real Elliot”, just like “Mr. Robot” was. She calls him the “Mastermind Elliot” who took care of all the hacks. Gloria pushes him to give back control to “real Elliot”. Finally, Elliot wakes up in a hospital bed with Darleen by his side.

And this is where things really go side-ways from everything we’ve seen before: it may be subtle to some, but Darleen wore a quite different set of clothing - sneakers with white soles and a somewhat fashionable overthrow. On top of that, she had two rings on her hands. This is a clothing style in which we have never seen her before, already stating that this sequence is in some form of “other world”. Elliot then asks whether everything regarding Whiterose truly happened. Darleen cries and says it was all real, Whiterose has been defeated and the Deus Group as well. But Darleen also knows that she isn’t talking to the “real Elliot” (whom we have never laid eyes on throughout the entire show) but instead to the “Mastermind Elliot” who was the designer of all the hacks in the narrative. Therefore, my theory is that Darleen was lying about these hacks being a reality just to make the “Mastermind Elliot” with all his psychological issues feel better.

The revelation that “Mastermind Elliot” is not real and just distracting the real Elliot from living his life indicates to me that everything we have seen throughout the show was not a reality. Perhaps the first few episodes in Season 1 were reality, but as soon as we always see Elliot walking around in the same set of clothes (black hoodie, black shoes, dark jeans) he becomes a distinct personality, “Mastermind Elliot”, just like Mr. Robot.

Here is where I go full-blown crazy: I believe that Tyrell Wellick’s character is the real Elliot. His entire character is very mystique and does not occur a lot throughout the show after season 1. Beginning from season 2 there is always a big secret regarding his whereabouts and he never shows up too often. But he is of extreme importance, otherwise he wouldn’t occur throughout the show. Even the final episode involves him again, where he shoots and buries the “Mastermind Elliot”. And in the sequence with the therapist Gloria, in which she tells mastermind Elliot that he is not real and just some split personality from the “real Elliot”, we even hear Tyrell Wellick distinctly say “Bonsoir, Elliot” which he told sometime in season 2 on the telephone. And season 2 was also the season where we started to realise how unreliable Mastermind Elliot is as a narrator - may I just remind the viewer of Elliot’s stay at his mothers and his engagement with the leader of a darknet website which turned out to be a fantasy and a warped understanding of the fact that Elliot was actually imprisoned. My belief is that when Darleen finally sees the real Elliot in the very last scene of the show that she sees Tyrell Wellick’s character. In fact, I was waiting throughout this entire scene that we would soon see Martin Wallström (Tyrell’s actor) replace Elliot in the back-and-forth cuts between him and Darleen. I also think that in the closeup of Darleen’s and Elliot’s hand during this sequence that the arm we see is not the one of Rami Malek (Mastermind Elliot’s actor) but instead of Martin Wallström.

But this is what I enjoy so much about this show’s finale: It gives you food for thought and allows you to come up with your own understanding of its events. I would be excited to hear from others what they have to say - what is your conclusion?

item.title: mock-inspect: Mock network requests and observe how these requests were made
item.description:
This article is part of a series about the open-source Node.js testing library mock-inspect of which I am the main contributor and author. Check out the entire series here!

mock-inspect is an open-source library for Node.js which allows you to mock network requests in your tests and to inspect how these requests have been made in the application. Has the endpoint been called? Which request body and request headers did the application pass along? This knowledge can be used to create your very own assertions, entirely depending on your use case.

Since mock-inspect is a Node.js library, it can be used both for frontend as well as backend applications. In this blog post, we want to explore the use case in a frontend project using React and how we can elevate our tests thanks to mock-inspect.

Application Outline

To demonstrate the usage of mock-inspect, I have prepared an example frontend application which is inspired by a real-life use case, the tray.io marketing website. Users can fill out a form to request a specific type of demo for the product. When the form is submitted, an API endpoint is pinged with all the properties that the user supplied. The frontend application always calls the same endpoint, no matter which selection the user makes. That endpoint will return a 200 status code, no matter what. Once the frontend application has forwarded the properties, it displays a message which says that the user is going to receive an e-mail. Note that the e-mail is scheduled to be sent; it could also be sent a few hours later.

A recording of the user flow:

Below is the React code for this simple application (feel free to skip over this one):

import "./App.css"
import {useState} from "react"
function App() {
const [name, setName] = useState("")
const [email, setEmail] = useState("")
const [demoType, setDemoType] = useState("")
const [submitted, setSubmitted] = useState(false)
const nameHandler = (event) => {
setName(event.target.value)
}
const emailHandler = (event) => {
setEmail(event.target.value)
}
const radioButtonHandler = (event) => {
setDemoType(event.type)
}
const forwardInputs = async () => {
const dataToSend = {name, email, demoType}
const response = await fetch("https://someapi.com/request-demo", {
method: "POST",
headers: {
"Content-Type": "application/json",
},
body: JSON.stringify(dataToSend),
})
if (response.status === 200) {
setSubmitted(true)
}
}
const getDemoForm = () => {
return (
<div className="App-header">
<input type="text" placeholder="Your name" onChange={nameHandler} />
<input type="text" placeholder="Your e-mail" onChange={emailHandler} />
<label htmlFor="live">
Live Demo
<input
type="radio"
id="live"
value="live"
onChange={radioButtonHandler}
/>
</label>
<label htmlFor="recorded">
Recorded Demo
<input
type="radio"
id="recorded"
value="recorded"
onChange={radioButtonHandler}
/>
</label>
<label htmlFor="1-2-1">
1-2-1 with a representative
<input
type="radio"
id="1-2-1"
value="1-2-1"
onChange={radioButtonHandler}
/>
</label>
<br />
<input type="button" value="Submit" onClick={forwardInputs} />
</div>
)
}
const getThankYouPage = () => {
return (
<div className="App-header">
Thank you! We will send you a confirmation e-mail.
</div>
)
}
return (
<div className="App">{!submitted ? getDemoForm() : getThankYouPage()}</div>
)
}
export default App

How do we test this? In order to make our unit tests stable, we will want to mock the network request so that the tests work independently from the real-world application. Many mocking tools will advise that a good test should check if the application correctly consumes the API response. But as we have seen in the use case description, there is nothing for our frontend application to consume - the API will return a 200 status code no matter what! In essence, all our application does is forwarding some properties. This is precisely why designing our test around the consumption of the API response would be too simple; we would not test enough by merely testing whether the application reacts to the 200 status code. What is actually important about our use case is that the frontend forwards the correct properties and in the correct format.

Let’s start writing a test for our application. As a start, we are going to use the approach of checking that the application consumes the API response. Below is a test for this in which we are already using mock-inspect to set up the request mock. Apart from that, we are using React Testing Library to enter text into the inputs and to select our desired demo type.

import {render, screen, fireEvent, waitFor} from "@testing-library/react"
import App from "./App"
import {mockRequest} from "mock-inspect"
test("shows Thank You page on submitting the form", async () => {
mockRequest({
requestPattern: "https://someapi.com/request-demo",
requestMethod: "POST",
responseStatus: 200,
})
render(<App />)
const nameInput = screen.getByPlaceholderText("Your name")
fireEvent.change(nameInput, {target: {value: "Han Solo"}})
const emailInput = screen.getByPlaceholderText("Your e-mail")
fireEvent.change(emailInput, {target: {value: "i-shot-first@coruscant.sw"}})
const recordedRadio = screen.getByLabelText("Recorded Demo")
fireEvent.click(recordedRadio)
const submitButton = screen.getByDisplayValue("Submit")
expect(submitButton).toBeInTheDocument()
fireEvent.click(submitButton)
await waitFor(() => {
expect(submitButton).not.toBeInTheDocument()
const thankYouPage = screen.getByText(
"Thank you! We will send you a confirmation e-mail.",
)
expect(thankYouPage).toBeInTheDocument()
})
})

Great, this test passes! Job done, right? After all, our application is forwarding the request payload of {"demoType": "recorded", "name:" "Han Solo", "email": "i-shot-first@coruscant.sw"} to the API. Well, … it doesn’t. Our test let a bug slip: instead of forwarding "demoType": "recorded", it sends "demoType": "change". Why is that? This is caused by a problem with the handler function of the radio buttons. We made a human error and sent event.type instead of event.target.value, probably because we were too focused on the word “type” in the setDemoType function. But we can fix this:

 // Inside App.js
const radioButtonHandler = (event) => {
- setDemoType(event.type)
+ setDemoType(event.target.value)
 }

But our test still doesn’t confirm whether the right properties are forwarded. Yes, we land on the Thank You page if we get a 200 status code from the backend; but we don’t know whether we send the right things. This is where mock-inspect comes to the rescue.

Whenever you mock a network request using mock-inspect’s mockRequest method, you receive a class instance. This instance contains a few useful methods, such as the method expectRequestToHaveBeenMade() which would throw an error if the application hasn’t made the request. Another useful method is inspect() which will return the request payload and the request headers which have been used by the application when it made the request. Should the request not have been made at the time of calling inspect(), the method would throw an error (as it internally calls expectRequestToHaveBeenMade()).

Thanks to the inspect() method, we can complete our test and assert that the frontend application does indeed forward the correct properties. Now we really can be sure everything works as expected!

test("shows Thank You page on submitting the form", async () => {
- mockRequest({
+ const demoRequest = mockRequest({
 requestPattern: "https://someapi.com/request-demo",
requestMethod: "POST",
responseStatus: 200,
})
render(<App />)
const nameInput = screen.getByPlaceholderText("Your name")
fireEvent.change(nameInput, {target: {value: "Han Solo"}})
const emailInput = screen.getByPlaceholderText("Your e-mail")
fireEvent.change(emailInput, {target: {value: "i-shot-first@coruscant.sw"}})
const recordedRadio = screen.getByLabelText("Recorded Demo")
fireEvent.click(recordedRadio)
const submitButton = screen.getByDisplayValue("Submit")
expect(submitButton).toBeInTheDocument()
fireEvent.click(submitButton)
await waitFor(() => {
expect(submitButton).not.toBeInTheDocument()
const thankYouPage = screen.getByText(
"Thank you! We will send you a confirmation e-mail.",
)
expect(thankYouPage).toBeInTheDocument()
})
+ // is also called inside `inspect()` - here for clarity
+ demoRequest.expectRequestToHaveBeenMade()
+ const {requestBody} = demoRequest.inspect()
+ expect(requestBody).toEqual({
+ name: "Han Solo",
+ email: "i-shot-first@coruscant.sw",
+ demoType: "recorded",
+ })
})

Conclusion

We have seen how mock-inspect can help you write better and more meaningful tests. If you want to learn more about the library, head over to our GitHub page where you can find detailed instructions on how to use mock-inspect. There, we also outline other functionalities we haven’t discussed yet, such as how to handle GraphQL, how to mock based on “example requests” and how to auto-generate responses using a GraphQL schema!

Keep an eye out for other upcoming articles in this series. In future blog posts, we will look at how mock-inspect works behind the scenes and how it can be used to facilitate contract testing.

Chameleon graphic by Анна Куликова from Pixabay

item.title: Windows Subsystem for Linux
item.description:
I have been using the Linux subsystem on Windows for a while now and I am quite thrilled about it. I do think that this is something everyone who is doing development on a Windows computer should set up.

What the Windows Subsystem for Linux is (and what it isn’t)

For a few years now, thanks to Windows 10, it has been possible to have a Linux subsystem available on your Windows operating system (often referred to as WSL). It is important to note that this just exposes a shell - you are still regularly interacting with Windows. Effectively, you just have an app that launches a Linux terminal. There is therefore no way to use graphical Linux tools such as video software and the Linux subsystem is also not a virtual machine.

Why would you want to use it? At work, I am using a Mac which is a UNIX operating system just like Linux. At least in my line of work (web development, testing and DevOps) developing on Linux is extremely common because this is usually the target operating system on which our programs run. Furthermore, there are a lot of command-line tools out there for Linux which don’t have a Windows equivalent. And even if these exist, it is simply nicer to work with the tools one already knows. This goes especially for scripting: bash/sh is a powerful scripting language and I have gotten quite good at it over the years - the same can not be said about my scripting skills with Windows and .bat files. I have a few projects (like my website) where I wrote node.js scripts to automate build tasks just because I couldn’t use bash and struggled with .bat files.

Yes, I could have a second operating system on my computer which would eliminate the need for a subsystem - but this comes with a different set of problems. In fact, I do have an installation of elementary OS on my computer and have dabbled with it for years but never managed to iron out the problems. The biggest hurdle is that I have a widescreen monitor but a very old graphics card. Getting Linux to display in full widescreen format is a task I could not achieve even after days of configuration - and judging from the hundreds of forum posts out there I am not alone with this.

My Windows dependent situation is amplified by the fact that my eyes need the app f.lux which takes away the eye-straining blue light elements. There is also a Linux version but it is far inferior to the Windows and Mac pendants. The final nail in the coffin was that I have an extensive set of AutoHotkey scripts to automate dozens of tasks with keyboard shortcuts - which is Windows only. On top of that, in order to switch operating systems while booting, I have to activate the BIOS mode on my Corsair keyboard as it otherwise only works once the keyboard driver installed on the operating system kicks in.

Installation and Setup

Installing the subsystem was relatively easy, there is a comprehensive guide on the Microsoft website. The word “relative” should be pointed out here as it is not as easy as installing let’s say a web browser - but hey, you are diving into the world of Linux. Get used to pain! Once you have the subsystem installed, you will have an app called “Ubuntu” available in your start menu which opens a Linux shell. All the standard shell commands work there, such as uname -r which shows the name of the Linux installation. In my case, this is 4.4.0-19041-Microsoft.

$ uname -r
4.4.0-19041-Microsoft

By default, the standard Ubuntu shell is rather unpleasing, both visually as well as functionally. Therefore your next first step should be to install zsh. I found a wonderful article by Joao Grassi which goes into great detail on how to set it up. An important step here is to use the Windows application ConEmu which is the Windows equivalent to iTerm2 - a comfortable shell window manager which many features such as splitting windows.

Usage and Development

Let’s get to the fun bit - developing with Linux on your Windows machine. Installing programming languages and tools works like on Ubuntu - don’t forget, you are using Ubuntu now! Thus, there is a lot of apt-get involved as well as downloading tools via curl and moving them into /usr/bin (or adding them to the $PATH variable on each shell start). This is quite pleasant since most tutorials and Getting Started guides are usually written for UNIX systems. I’m sure every Windows developer has been in a situation at least once where things errored on installing - no such problems with WSL.

While you are in the Linux subsystem, you have access to all the files on your Windows computer using the /mnt directory. For example, my Windows directory C:\code can be accessed from within the Linux subsystem on /mnt/c/code.

I have all my programming languages installed in WSL but some of these I also have installed on Windows, i.e. Golang. This is extremely useful because that way I can compile programs both for Linux and for Windows on the same computer.

Visual Studio Code for Windows Linux Subsystems

Having a separate Linux shell is great but you don’t want to do everything in the terminal, especially when it comes to writing code. Sure, some people love vim but most of us are probably dependent on actual text editors and IDEs. This is where Visual Studio Code comes heroically to the rescue with its incredible Remote WSL extension. Once this extension is installed, you can launch Visual Studio Code on Windows like you usually would - but VSCode will run on your Linux subsystem!

The important point here is that the entire process of VSCode will run on the Linux subsystem. What this means in action: For example, when you are installing language formatters such as for Golang, these will go into your Linux subshell. For me, this is ground-breaking because it means that I can for the first time comfortably develop in Golang using Visual Studio Code. Many of the tools used by the Golang VSCode extension did not work properly on Windows. For years, I was reluctantly living with these problems. But now when using Linux, these formatters and tools suddenly all work as they should. It’s incredible.

In summary, the Windows subsystem for Linux is a fantastic addition that should be a strong consideration of every Windows developer’s toolchain. While the setup process is not easy, it is worth it. My prediction is that it will find a wide adoption over the next few years and will only advance the strong position UNIX operating systems have in the development community.

item.title: Until Dawn - Review
item.description:
I am delighted to talk about this “game” - because Until Dawn is an experience which took me by surprise, mostly due to its clever writing.

But first, let’s get out of the way what type of game Until Dawn is - or rather what type of game it is not. Until Dawn belongs into the genre of interactive movies; comparable titles are Heavy Rain, Beyond: Two Souls and Detroit: Become Human. I started the two latter games but never finished them. I grew bored with these and felt dissatisfied by how few game elements there were. The reason I gave Until Dawn nonetheless a chance was that somebody recommended and also borrowed it to me.

While other games of this genre don’t make it clear what type of experience they are, Until Dawn somewhat indicates it with its cast. I did not know going in that Rami Malek was in it, but when I fired the game up I recognised him immediately as well as Hayden Panettiere, another well-known actress. The presence of these A-list actors made it obvious that it wanted to be regarded as an interactive TV show rather than a game. The fact that Rami Malek stars in a Playstation game still baffles me, considering that he went on to star as the lead in the TV show Mr Robot the same year of release and went on to win an Academy Award a bit later.

Coming back to the “game”: Until Dawn is an interactive TV show in the style of modern gore horror movies. Eight teenagers (although none of the actors was in their teens, most male actors were even already in their 30s) meet for a weekend away in a remote lodge on top of a mountain while a winter storm is howling. Spooky and gory events are as much to be expected as are horny innuendos and shy regards.

The game’s novelty stems from its choice system, the so-called butterfly effects: Many of your choices throughout the game affect the narrative - most of them minor, but some of them actually in a major way. In fact, there is a chance that all eight teens survive the night - or none of them. Up to the middle point of the game, I was underwhelmed by the system because many of the outcomes were in the style of “You sided with character A against character B - later on, character B was angry with you.” I wouldn’t have been surprised if one outcome would have been “You chose to take out the trash - the trash stood outside in the next scene”. But this should be expected in a game of such quality, the studio can’t create hundreds of different stories; the plot still follows all the main beats no matter your choices.

The events that drastically have an effect are relating to death or survival: I was researching on YouTube after my playthrough about all the possible outcomes and I am floored by the many ways in which the story can play out. Each of your characters has multiple ways they can die and this affects subsequent scenes since entire characters are missing. Not all of these deaths relate to your choices though; many deaths can be caused by bad reactions in the game’s quick-time events. As a player, this can seem frustrating and unfair. But on the other hand, it makes for a unique experience: there is no “Game Over” screen, the story continues even if you fail. Whether you win or lose actually has meaning.

I am fascinated by the idea of making a video game with such a great cast which fully intends to closely resemble a TV show. I wonder what the financial implications of this are: Is it cheaper to create a video game than a TV show? Which can attract a wider audience?

Not all is gold and roses though with Until Dawn and I have to mention it: Technically, it is a catastrophe. The graphics are fantastic, even more so for a game from 2015, it can easily be called photorealistic. But the framerate is inexcusable; even on a Playstation 4 Pro (a system which didn’t exist yet when this game came out), the game stutters noticeably, especially in fast-paced action scenes but also during simple walking situations. Almost worse is the sound: the entire audio mix is highly uneven (jump scares are incredibly loud compared to the dialogue, even after readjusting volumes in the settings) and the dialogue sounds like it was recorded with a smartphone. I do wonder what happened here during development. If you have Academy Award-winning stars in your cast, having such a bad sound which completely muffles their performances is simply inexcusable.

I do recommend giving this interactive TV show a try, it is just £7.99 during sale events. Just don’t expect every butterfly effect to be ground-breaking or to receive grandiose gameplay elements; otherwise you will enjoy this as well!

item.title: Doom Eternal - Review
item.description:
Oh boy did I have a blast with Doom Eternal. Rarely have I played a game so intense that my eyes were regularly fluttering after a gaming session.

I presume there is not a lot of introduction necessary for the type of Doom Eternal’s gameplay: it is a first-person shooter which has you tear through hordes of hell demons.

The impressive thing about it is how the shooter mechanics are scaled back to the absolute essentials. There is no aim down sight feature, no weapon reloading - the game has you shooting to your heart’s desire with little restrictions. The developers understood well what makes a shooter fun and they are committed to giving you as much of this as possible.

The movement mechanics are as focused as the shooting counterpart: there is no unnecessary crouching or, god forbid, leaning around edges. Doom Eternal has you racing and strafing through the battlefields in speeds which can only be described as neck-breaking, especially when you unlock abilities which increase movement speed after melee kills or when you use the dash ability to propel from place to place like a tiny rocket ship. There is also a horizontal component to movement as you can double jump to your heart’s content. I was pleasantly reminded of multiplayer titles like the excellent Unreal Tournament.

In a way, Doom Eternal is also a strategic game: many of the enemies have specific weak points which you can target: one enemy has a protective shield which is easily destroyed with the plasma gun; another shoots rockets from a backpack which you can destroy to disable them. Thus, I found myself using almost all the weapons regularly depending on the situation. (I say almost - Ballista, you suck.) Ammunition is, especially in the beginning, relatively tight and runs out fast. You can replenish ammo by literally chainsawing through smaller demons though.

The difficulty level is a very interesting topic: I played Doom Eternal on Normal and found the challenge in the beginning to be quite hefty. My problem was that I was not yet used to the game’s mechanics and felt overwhelmed by all the capabilities the Doom Slayer possesses and which you all have to use to survive: you can melee enemies to stagger them, you have two types of grenades, there is a flame thrower which you need to spray at enemies to get shield points, you have a chainsaw, later on even a sword… the options are plenty. But once you mastered these mechanics you feel like an absolute machine. In the final sections, I was tearing myself through even the largest hordes with relative ease.

Another great thing I have to mention is how the game handles boss fights: I usually suck at boss fights in video games and also don’t enjoy them. It annoys me having to evade attacks and being forced to play by their rules instead of resorting to my own play style. Of course, this pattern is still a given in Doom Eternal, but the game offers you an increased shield in boss fights should you die too often - it does so without any judgement and the buff does not even hurt progression or rewards. While I only had to use this feature once in the penultimate boss fight, I felt incredibly relieved that they thought about this and also offered it so freely. Because let’s be honest, who enjoys repeating bosses multiple times?

Visually, the game is stunning on my Playstation 4 Pro. This is even more impressive given how fast the gameplay is and how many enemies are on screen during fights. Doom Eternal has a very distinct style which can be best compared to sleeves of 70s Heavy Metal albums. The environments are relatively distinct (there is a destroyed city, the planet Mars, an ice world, and even your own private castle which is a fricking spaceship!) and they all look pretty gloomy which delightfully contrasts with the flashy colours of the demons, explosions and collectables. It should be odd that this mix fits, but it does.

One thing which also has to be mentioned is the music which is once again composed by Mick Gordon and fits the game fantastically. It is an odd mix of heavy electronic beats and incredibly chunky guitar riffs which make me as an avid metalcore fan extremely happy.

Of course, even an excellent game has some drawbacks and in the case of Doom Eternal this is the enemy called Marauder. This enemy appears not too frequently (luckily) but is hard to kill: due to his attacks, you can neither be too far nor too close him. When he attacks you, there is a split second in which you can shoot to counter him. The annoying bit about this is that the window you have to react is very narrow, at least for me - perhaps my reaction time isn’t the fastest. Also, it takes a lot of shots to kill him. This makes the Marauder incredibly annoying. It seems like the developers knew this as well because whenever he appears, there aren’t a lot of other enemies around except for small fodder demons through which help you retrieve ammunition - of which you need a lot to defeat the Marauder.

You might have noticed that I didn’t say too much about the story - because there isn’t any. Essentially, hell demons enslave almost all of humanity and you have to stop them. I enjoyed how serious the story took itself, the game doesn’t try to crack jokes: Doom Eternal is all about trying to deliver this heavy metal fantasy that you play the most badass killing machine in the universe. Jokes and giggles would only clash with this vision.

Doom Eternal is a fantastic game and I can wholeheartedly recommend it. I bought it for £15 and I am almost inclined to get the DLC which is £15.99, more than the main game cost me - but I had that much fun with it that I think it might actually be worth it.

item.title: Desperados III - Review
item.description:
It’s about time I am reviewing Desperados III - what a game! Without this, my last week of 2020 would have looked very different. This game brought pride when I succeeded in its challenges - and anger when I failed.

Desperados III belongs to the Real-Time Strategy genre - this means that the player controls their characters from the top-down perspective and has to try to eliminate (or evade) enemies unseen. It’s essentially a stealth game from a bird’s eye view.

You can display each enemy’s view cone and have to evade these - otherwise everyone nearby will be alarmed. The essentially simple objective becomes incredibly difficult given the way enemies are positioned: they often appear in big numbers and stand in circles and other shapes so that each of your enemies has a good view onto another person. In order to succeed, you can lure people away i.e. by whistling or making them turn around to where a coin was thrown. These short moments are where you can navigate your characters to strike and eliminate the threat. That way, you chomp yourself step by step through even the biggest masses.

What makes this so difficult is that the R in RTS stands for real time - you don’t have a lot of time to react. When the moment is right, you have to strike. Yes, there is a pause mode where you can plan actions for multiple characters, but apart from certain actions, you can always just plan one task per team member. And even if you did plan meticulously, your crew still has to execute your instructions - thus it could very well be that you send them off to point A which you assumed safe in pause mode only to discover that once the action continues, the route is actually not safe anymore because an enemy turned around.

Opponents can be eliminated in various ways: you could either walk up to them and kill them from behind, you could throw a knife, shoot, have one of your characters distract them through flirting or blind them - the options are wide! Since I am so used to “pure” stealth games like Splinter Cell, it initially felt wrong to me to use guns though. Instead, I resorted to melee kills and knife throws. But this really diminished my success and I realised later on that there is only so much one can do without a bullet. It turns out that the habit of not shooting is actually hard to unlearn! During my playthrough, I often forced my way through encounters employing close-combat kills although guns would have done the job much easier and saved me a lot of pain.

Failure is encouraged by the game though, you can always quicksave and reload with the press of a button. The recap of each mission often showed me that the number of saves and loads easily went into the hundreds. And this brings me to my main gripe with the game: I love quick saving and reloading, it helps me perfecting my stealth techniques and getting an execution 100% right. But I don’t think the existence of a quick save feature should be an excuse for not testing your game - and I have the impression that the developers mostly play-tested themselves instead of observing real people. Most of the situations to defeat enemies require absolute millimetre work and cat-like timing - even doing one step just slightly wrong will already cause you to fail. This forces you to reload sections over and over although what you were doing and planning was already great - it’s just that you didn’t pick exactly the correct millisecond or click exactly the right pixel. This invites frustration.

The levels in Desperados III are extremely open and the ways you can eliminate your targets are highly variable, different players will do it differently. While this is a great thing, I suspect the developers did not consider at all how one could beat the mission when designing the levels and placing the characters - “They’ll beat it somehow, don’t worry”. To me, this is especially obvious given that the enemies' view cones are always the same size and length no matter the situation. I think the view cones should have been adjusted individually depending on the circumstances of each mission.

Enough with the complaints though, because it is all in all an excellent game. I highly enjoyed the Western atmosphere and the banter between characters. The 16 missions (each between 1 and 2 hours of playtime) all had me on their toes and I never felt bored. The game is at its best when you have all five of your characters available, but unfortunately, there is only one full mission where you get the chance to use all of your crew members for its entirety. This also makes for a lot of variety though and you get to know each of your crew’s skills and drawbacks as you can’t always resort to using the same person.

Probably one of the best things about the game: Desperados III even runs on pretty old PCs like mine. There is also a console version available but I frankly cannot imagine how this would work. I strongly required large screen resolution as well as keyboard and mouse to hand out my meticulous orders. On a console, I would probably have given up on it because I would have felt limited by the game.

Games like these which completely suck you into their mechanics are rare - I think Desperados III is a must-play. There were nights where I honestly dreamed about character positionings and days where I imagined I would see view cones on real-life passers-by. Thanks for giving me a great send-off to a bad 2020!

item.title: How to make a blog such as this one with Hugo
item.description:
Creating a new article in this blog is a matter of writing a new text file and pushing it to my GitHub repository - while keeping it 100% free. Here’s how I achieved it to be this simple.

  1. How to generate the website
  2. How to host the website

How to generate the website

I use the tool Hugo to create this blog. Hugo is a static site generator - this means that you simply feed it with some text files and it then spits out all of the files which form a complete web page. Although Hugo is written in Go, it is published as a CLI tool - there is no Go knowledge necessary to use it.

Setting up the website was fairly easy, I literally followed the Quick Start guide in the docs. There are a few things which I did differently and I want to outline these to make it easier on people who also want to start their own blog.

Hugo is based around the concept of templates - instead of you having to create your own website from scratch, you can access other people’s page templates and make them your own. Anyone can create templates and there are literally hundreds of them for free on their website. This is how I found the Anubis theme. As I wanted to tweak this theme and adjust it to my personal needs, I created a GitHub fork of the project. If you have a theme you consider using, I would highly advise you to also fork it right away as it gravely simplifies the later modification process. Once the theme was forked, I had to pull it in as a git submodule: git submodule add https://github.com/rickschubert/hugo-theme-anubis themes/anubis.

From there on, working with Hugo is a charm: hugo new posts/my-new-post.md will create a new markdown file in the directory posts where all the blog posts live. In case you haven’t seen markdown before, this is literally how the start of this article looks like!

---
title: "How to make a blog such as this one with Hugo"
date: 2021-01-01T15:29:34Z
draft: true
tags: ["programming"]
---
I am really happy with the tools I use for my blog [...]
I use the tool [Hugo](https://gohugo.io/) to create this blog.

Since the articles are markdown, writing new posts becomes a breeze. I write all of my articles in Visual Studio Code with its amazing “Zen Mode” feature. Trust me, I wrote a full play with this program - it’s amazing! Since all posts are plain text, I can copy and paste them wherever necessary; I often move my texts to grammarly.com in order to perform a quick spell-check. (I am the king of typos, I know.)

If all is done, the static website files can be generated using hugo --minify which creates a directory public/ - easy as that!

How to host the website

My hosting solution is fully automated and 100% free - two attributes I am incredibly happy about. I use the GitHub pages feature which makes it possible to transform GitHub repositories into websites. I am using this feature already for my main page https://rickschubert.net which actually lives under https://rickschubert.github.io (as rickschubert is my GitHub user name). I then updated the DNS configuration of my domain to point to the GitHub server.

If you already have one repository that is your main GitHub page, any other repository which you want to host via GitHub pages becomes accessible as path with the name of the repository - so a repository /blog would be accessible under https://rickschubert.github.io/blog. But since I have the DNS redirects set up over at my domain provider Google Domains, the redirects also work for the blog!

My workflow of bringing the files to GitHub is fairly ingenious. While https://github.com/rickschubert/blog contains the minified and uglified static website files which need to be open to the public, the actual source code of the website is stored in a private repository. That repository has GitHub actions configured in such a way that on every push to that private repository, the hugo --minify command gets run to build the static website. Another task then moves these output files to my /blog repository which is open to the public. See below my GitHub actions file (.github/workflows/hugo.yml) for how I achieve this:

name: Website publish
on:
push:
branches: [ master ]
jobs:
deploy:
runs-on: ubuntu-18.04
steps:
- uses: actions/checkout@v2
with:
submodules: true # Fetch Hugo themes (true OR recursive)
fetch-depth: 0 # Fetch all history for .GitInfo and .Lastmod
- name: Setup Hugo
uses: peaceiris/actions-hugo@v2
with:
hugo-version: '0.79.1'
- name: Build
run: HUGO_ENV=production hugo --minify
- name: Pushes to another repository
uses: cpina/github-action-push-to-another-repository@master
env:
API_TOKEN_GITHUB: ${{ secrets.API_TOKEN_GITHUB }}
with:
source-directory: 'public'
destination-github-username: 'rickschubert'
destination-repository-name: 'blog'
user-email: rickschubert@gmx.de
target-branch: gh-pages

On the Hugo website, the tag line reads “Hugo makes building websites fun again” - I have to agree! The concept of shareable themes takes all the pain from content producers, especially for small projects such as this blog. Instead of having to spend countless hours designing and updating my website I get the chance to focus on writing articles. Thanks, Hugo!

item.title: Volunteering With My Programming Skills For A Non-Commercial Radio Station
item.description:
During the Crimbo Limbo period between Christmas and New Years, I volunteered with my programming skills. That’s how it came to be that I built a tool to create a browsable music library for the non-commercial radio station Great American Songbook. This article discusses why and how.

  1. How I came to volunteer
  2. Building the tool
  3. Final thoughts

How I came to volunteer

For me, programming is like an itch. If I haven’t programmed in a while I start to feel fidgety. Furthermore, I am deeply passionate about automation and eliminating bullshit jobs. This was actually how I started programming in the first place - back in my time when I was a linguist there had been dozens of manual tasks multiple people (including me) performed over and over. I got so annoyed by it that I taught myself how to program in order to get rid of these.

Volunteering was something I had considered already for a long time. I have approached multiple organisations over the past year, i.e. local hospitals at the height of coronavirus pressures. Most of the time, I either never heard back or have been told there was no need for help. It is surprising through how many hoops one has to jump just to work for free!

The Great American Songbook is a radio station I have been listening to for many years now. Swing music and dance are big passions for me. I donated some money to that radio station and that’s how I thought again about volunteering: couldn’t I donate my time and skills additionally to money? That’s how I contacted the radio station and almost instantly received an answer.

During a zoom call, the radio producer walked me through the website’s setup and how the radio stream operates. In my personal circle, I don’t have many people of age and no one who could let alone operate a tablet. Therefore, I was incredibly impressed by the radio producer who is a pensioner and runs the radio station completely out of their own pocket. They have a setup of multiple servers, a website with SSH certificate, templates from GitHub, site updates using FTP, … - and they browsed through their media library using FreeCommander like a champ!

Our initial conversation immediately lay bare automation needs: On the website, there is a section which lists the complete music library. That way, listeners know what songs are available (and which aren’t) so that they can request songs to be played more frequently or ask for new songs to be added to the rotation. Creating this PDF was a constant long-lasting pain to the radio producer, they only did it a few times a year because it took so long. This is where my tool comes in!

Building the tool

The code for this project is open-source and can be found on GitHub.

The task at hand was to crawl through the entire music collection of the radio producer and to generate a sorted list of all the tracks. This list should be sorted either by the artist or title. It should also be possible to exclude subdirectories from the generated catalogue.

It was clear to me that I wanted to have the catalogue displayed in an interactive table using HTML so that listeners can filter and sort easily. I also decided to add CSV export functionality to my tool so that the radio producer could still convert the list into a PDF if needed.

I wanted to build a tool which could be executed by the radio producer without any of my input - that’s what automation is about after all: empowering the user. This is why I decided to use the Go programming language. Although I’m an expert in JavaScript/TypeScript, it is not a great solution for tools that should be executed by people/machines outside your control. I wouldn’t want to give the radio producer a list of things they need to install and commands they need to execute! With Go, I can compile the tool into one single executable file which the radio producer can start with a simple double click as they would do with any other program.

Once I knew all these requirements, I immediately started programming. I created a small command-line tool using the prompter library. I decided against any command-line flags and arguments as the tool was supposed to be used by non-techy people as well with just a double click. Therefore, I think it is way better to use a solution like prompter which asks the user for their input. That way, you can also give the user hints and examples. Unlike with avoidable --help messages, these prompts will definitely be seen by the user, there is no way around them. The prompter library is also incredibly easy to use:

func promptForDirectory() string {
directory := prompter.Prompt(
"Please provide us with the path of the " +
"directory where all your music lies. I.e. C:\\Music",
""
)
if directory == "" {
log.Fatal("You need to enter a valid path")
}
return directory
}

One of the trickiest bits for me was to recursively iterate over all directories and subdirectories. I use the filepath.Walk() function which already works recursively out of the box! Due to my familiarity with JavaScript, I hadn’t expected this though and assumed subdirectories were ignored. This is why I initially created unnecessary duplication where I would recursively call filepath.Walk again for every subdirectory - madness!

Golang is a highly readable programming language if done well - it requires the user to abstract things out a bit though. Consider the below code where I I try to determine whether a file path (path) starts with any item of the ignoredDirectories list. The code itself is quite readable but I have it placed in the midst of another function - I should have created a helper function called Find() instead. We live to make mistakes 🤷.

// This should be its own function, something like `Find()`
var shouldBeIgnored bool
for _, ignoredDirectory := range ignoredDirectories {
if ignoredDirectory != "" {
if strings.HasPrefix(strings.ToLower(path), strings.ToLower(ignoredDirectory)) {
shouldBeIgnored = true
break
}
}

Reading out artist name etc. from MP3 files was fairly straight-forward thanks to the tag library. The same goes for creating CSV files which works with the built-in library encoding/csv.

More interesting - and challenging - was creating the catalogue as HTML table. Some people surely wonder whether I went with some grand solution like a serverless database and API calls to fetch items - way too complicated! Remember our goals: replacing the PDF file and empowering the radio producer. We wouldn’t want to teach them about database access, requiring them to host things, or me hosting things for them which might not work any longer if I don’t maintain it. Instead, I went for a simple and proven solution: Bake all the songs as table rows into a static HTML file.

The main idea of this tool was to have one single executable to give the radio producer. Therefore, having HTML mustache templates was out of the question as well. This is why I ended up hard-coding an actual string into my .go file which represents the base HTML skeleton. This skeleton is then enriched with all the table rows for each song where each item is converted into a <tr> element.

I initially created my own basic HTML table including custom filter functions but I was shocked by the atrocious performance of my naive scripts. The Great American Songbook music library contains 16.000 items - due to that size there is a need for smart filters. This is how I stumbled over the DataTables project which provides a fantastic out of the box solution for displaying tables, including pagination and filtering. Even on tables with 16.000 rows the search and sorting functionality works tremendously fast and stable.

While the filtering is fast, I had to do some trickery in regards to page loads. Because all of the <tr> elements are in the HTML page, the final page is 1.48MB large. This is a good decrease in size compared to the PDF (2.5MB) but it’s still quite large to load for browsers. It turns out that if there are more than 16.000 elements on the page, your browser has to do quite a lot of work to process all of these items. This is why I had a look around and used the solution from patdavid.net where I first hide the table on page load. Instead, the site is showing a loading spinner. Once all elements are processed by the browser and in the DOM, I hide the loading spinner and display the table. I am using the jQuery event method $(document).ready() for that.

The end result works pretty well and the radio producer was very happy about it - what previously took hours became fully automated and finished in around 60 seconds!

Implementing it into the webpage

The radio producer told me that they would be able to implement the solution as iframe on the website - which was why I went with a separate HTML file to begin with. After a few hours, they contacted me though to ask whether I could help out integrating it. Of course I could!

Implementing the iframe into the existing website was quite tricky. The main problem was that I had to wrap my head around the website’s framework which the radio producer purchased from another developer. While greatamericansongbook.info is a single-page application, there aren’t any frameworks being used, it’s all vanilla JavaScript: everything in jQuery, all scripts somewhat minified (highly shortened variable and function names but at least line breaks and indentation left intact). Another problem was that the website doesn’t use any version control - it’s just a bunch of files on an FTP server.

Another problem was that I needed to have the iframe only loaded when the user requires to see it. As I discussed earlier, the HTML file for the table is very large. If you have an iframe element in your HTML file with a src attribute pointing to another page your browser will also load that other page though - even if the iframe isn’t immediately visible to users. This is why the page loads were suddenly quite slow. I solved it by removing the src attribute from the iframe element in the initial HTML file. I managed to find in the source files the function which is being called whenever the URL changes. This is how I added another jQuery listener which only fires when the URL contains #!/page_LIBRARY_TABLE - in that case, I am adding to the iframe the src attribute leading to our big HTML table. Thus, the browser only loads our big table once it’s necessary.

The final product looks something like this (might not be on the website yet, development version):

Final thoughts

This was a fun and surprisingly challenging project which was right up my alley. I love that programming allows me to empower people: the radio producer doesn’t have to spend hours anymore having to create a PDF library and the listeners can browse the entire catalogue with ease, allowing them to become more engaged.

Volunteering was a lot of fun and is something which I will want to do again. As outlined above, it isn’t always that easy to find suitable projects. I think I got really lucky with how trusting and open the radio producer was. Even more so, they immediately grasped what I meant when I said “I do automation” and they had a fitting task for me in mind right away. Thank you very much!

item.title: Little Fires Everywhere - Book Review
item.description:
It is rare that a book comes along which makes me think so much about its themes and subjects. In that way, “Little Fires Everywhere” could be on track of becoming a modern classic.

It is one of those books where the characters feel completely life-like. For a novel grounded in the reality of the 1990s in the US, the plot is quite exciting and there are plenty of revelations and events which keep you on your toe. The story discusses the tensions between two different families and their opposing ideas of what a good life means. And tensions do run high towards the end of the book, which culminates in an explosive finale. But it bothered me a bit that there are so many conflicts opened up but few of them are actually followed through to their fullest. Many conflicts brood between the lines and remain unspoken. This seems like some missed opportunity.

It is a very feminine story, which I found highly refreshing - there aren’t a lot of male characters in this book, and if so these are only side characters. The only male person with an actual personality is teenage son Moody; but we know barely anything about all other male characters; certainly not how they feel, only what they do. Even son Trip, whose role is reasonably prominent, presents himself solely as a good-looking sportsman and this is also pretty much the only reason why teenage girl Pearl desires him. Some might argue that this is only a fair reversal of the usual tropes for literature romances; others might say it’s sexism in reverse. Be that as it may.

Many reviewers suggest that “Little Fires Everywhere” talks a lot about race but I have to vehemently disagree with this. One character in this book is Asian and one minor side character is black - how would that make this book explore race? A far bigger problem is that many of these critics and also most of the characters in the book - heck, even the narrator! - seem to confuse race with culture. What, so a person of Asian race needs to know about Chinese culture when they grow up in America? Does a black Frenchman have to know about “the customs of their people”? I find it deplorable that the issue of race relations has to be projected onto every work of art even if it’s completely unrelated. In the case of “Little Fires Everywhere”, I’m sure the author enjoys this line of thought as it makes for great publicity, but I absolutely fail to see how this book discusses or even just depicts the topic of race.

item.title: Flushing Promises in JavaScript - what it is and why you need it in UI tests
item.description:
“This UI test is flaky, why does it sometimes fail?” - “Could it be because promises were not flushed?” - “Huuuuh?” Chances are high that frontend developers will have had conversations like this. From my experience, many developers are still unaware of what flushing promises means and why we need it in automated tests of UI components such as with React or Angular. Let’s shed some light on this.

All the example code can be found on this GitHub repository.

Why do I need to flush promises?

Consider the following case: I created a new UI component. On starting, it displays the text “Empty”. When the component renders, it starts a network request to a server. The server takes 1 second to return a response. Once the component receives the response, it replaces the text “Empty” with the response it received.

Expand to see a demo GIF of the component:

The code of this dummy component:

import React from "react"
export async function makeNetworkRequest() {
const response = await fetch("http://localhost:3001/what-day-is-it")
const data = await response.json()
return JSON.stringify(data)
}
class App extends React.Component {
constructor(props) {
super(props)
this.state = {
webResponse: "Empty",
}
}
async componentDidMount() {
const webResponse = await makeNetworkRequest()
this.setState({webResponse})
}
render() {
return (
<div className="App">
<div className="response">{this.state.webResponse}</div>
</div>
)
}
}
export default App

This component works, fantastic! So let’s write some automated tests for this.

First, let’s test our network request function in isolation. We will use the npm module nock to mock the network request that would normally be made. This allows us to run the tests without our server. It will essentially fake the network request and have it always return a fake response, the JSON string {"A": "mocked response"}:

import App, {makeNetworkRequest} from "./App"
import nock from "nock"
const mockedWebResponse = JSON.stringify('{"A": "mocked response"}')
function setUpMock() {
nock(/.*/)
.persist()
.defaultReplyHeaders({
"access-control-allow-origin": "*",
"access-control-allow-credentials": "true",
})
.get(/what-day-is-it/)
.reply(200, mockedWebResponse)
}
it("Testing our network request", async () => {
setUpMock()
const result = await makeNetworkRequest()
expect(result).toEqual(mockedWebResponse)
})

This test passes, amazing. Take note that makeNetworkRequest is an asynchronous function. Since we are using the async and await keywords, nodejs will hold up with the subsequent I/O executions and only populate the result variable with a value once makeNetworkRequest finishes.

An I/O execution is any standard task which doesn’t perform asynchronously, i.e. adding up 2 + 2.

Let’s move on to testing our component. We will render the component using the Testing Library npm module and then check whether the text shows our fixed response:

import {render} from "@testing-library/react"
it("Test if component renders web response", () => {
const component = render(<App />)
const response = component.container.querySelector(".response")
expect(response.textContent).toEqual(mockedWebResponse)
})

But this test is failing. Why? Because no promises have been flushed. Alternatively, because nodejs exited too early. What jest does here is executing the function inside the it block which includes I/O operations only. But the update of the component where the text changes from “Empty” to our mocked response is not synchronous; this update happens in fact asynchronously because of our network request but also because of how React works internally. But since the test is not using any await keyword, nodejs exits as soon as possible. And this is the major problem with UI tests: Components are observables which don’t return promises or values. Therefore, we cannot add await keywords anywhere at will.

And this is why we need to flush promises. Let us first speak about how we could get the test to pass and we will then discuss why it works.

We can flush promises with the following function:

function flushPromises() {
return new Promise(setImmediate)
}

Since it returns a promise, it needs to be called using await:

it("Test if component renders web response", async () => {
const component = render(<App />)
const response = component.container.querySelector(".response")
for (let index = 0; index < 200; index++) {
await flushPromises()
}
expect(response.textContent).toEqual(mockedWebResponse)
})

And there you go, the test passes now! Alternatively, we could also use the waitFor helper exposed by the Testing Library. A deep-dive into the source code shows that it is essentially just a wrapper around our current solution which also uses setImmediate - but the waitFor helper doesn’t flush for a random 200 times but only as often as actually needed. This speeds up our test slightly.

import {render, waitFor} from "@testing-library/react"
it("Test if component renders web response", async () => {
const component = render(<App />)
const response = component.container.querySelector(".response")
await waitFor(() => expect(response.textContent).toEqual(mockedWebResponse))
})

How does flushing promises work

As you saw above, both solutions involved the function setImmediate. This function is special. In fact, the MDN Web Docs mention that it should not be used in production websites. But since we only deal here with nodejs and tests, this doesn’t matter to us.

In order to know more about how setImmediate works, we need to learn what the JavaScript event loop is. I will try my best to sum it up and paraphrase: JavaScript is a non-blocking programming language. This means that if I would make 10 network requests each taking 1 second then my program would not take 10 seconds but still only 1 second. This is achieved via the event loop which is essentially a task queue. JavaScript then takes the tasks from the task queue and executes them concurrently.

Imagine you are in school and your teacher gives you homework - you will not complete all of these tasks right the instance the teacher asked you to do so; instead, you will write down these tasks in your notebook and you will execute these tasks once it’s a convenient time to do so.

Sticking to the homework example, let’s pretend your teacher gave you 100 tasks of homework. But there is only so much you can do in one day, you only have time for 10 exercises per day. This is your “call stack size”. The nodejs event loop looks at all scheduled tasks and tries to put as many as possible onto the call stack so that they can be executed.

But here is where the function setImmediate comes in. If the teacher would have given 100 tasks for the next week, nodejs would try to prioritise the tasks which the teacher gave the longest time ago in order to catch up. But functions which are scheduled using setImmediate are special - these functions are additional tasks. This means that if your teacher gave you 100 tasks and 5 tasks scheduled using setImmediate, nodejs will first do the 10 tasks it can do per day and then additionally also do all of the tasks queued up using setImmediate. In essence, setImmediate functions are extra tasks which take higher priority.

How does this translate onto our flushPromises function? The above way of writing was implicit, so let’s pick it apart and write it explicitly:

function flushPromises() {
return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
setImmediate(() => {
resolve()
})
})
}

What happens is that an asynchronous event (all functions returning promises are async functions) is being scheduled which contains a setImmediate call. As we have learnt, setImmediate is only called in the event loop after tasks from the call stack have been executed. Therefore the event loop is forced to execute tasks of the call stack first before it can call setImmediate. Our setImmediate function is basically sandwiched into all of the other queued up tasks. Since we are returning a promise, we have again something to wait for using the await keyword.

In essence, because setImmediate always happens after the execution of tasks on the event loop, “flushing promises” is about forcing the event loop to execute some queued up tasks. But since the call stack is only limited (remember that we can only do 10 pieces of homework per day), we need to call it multiple times in order to force multiple cycles through the event loop.

And that’s flushing promises for you! I hope that this article both helped you understand the problem and also how to solve it.

item.title: Resident Evil Remake - Review
item.description:
I saw this game for £3.99 in the PSN store and since it’s the festive period with a lot of time on my hands (thanks Corona), I decided to give this a try. Previously, I was playing the recent remakes of Resident Evil 2 and 3 which I deeply enjoyed.

Why do I even enjoy the franchise? It is a mystery to me as well, I’m honest. In my childhood, I have always been afraid of horror movies and games. Even Terminator, which is in hindsight not the darkest movie in the world, would have given me nightmares. The impressive part of childhood me was that I did not even try out these movies or games - just the plain knowledge of their existence made me already feel ill at ease. “Watching a movie about an alien that kills people? Thanks, I’m headed for Nopetown!”

There are two things I greatly enjoy about the Resident Evil games: the puzzles and the deeply rooted 90s flair. I’m a sucker for this decade and there is something so visceral of it in this franchise that it’s almost too hard to grasp. It’s the corny dialogue, the cliché looking monsters and zombies, the lack of always ready communication, that weird attraction for handguns, the urban environments… Resident Evil screams 90s.

The Resident Evil I played is actually a 2015 released HD remaster of the game from 2002 - which in turn was a remake of the game from 1996. Surprisingly, it doesn’t feel like it would be almost 20 years old; more like 10 years. The graphics greatly benefit from hand-drawn backgrounds. Characters look relatively dated but this just underlines the 90s flair.

The gameplay is simple: The player navigates either Chris or Jill and has to find their way out of a creepy mansion filled with zombies. (I chose Jill, I have a thing for playing as female characters.) The game uses fixed camera angles while the player explores the mansion. When pressing a button, the character stands still and points their gun at an enemy. While doing so, you are able to shoot.

During the exploration of the mansion, players encounter many locked areas which can often be accessed by means of solving some puzzle - there is a lot of backtracking involved. Exploring the mansion was by far the aspect I enjoyed the most. I loved stumbling through this creepy environment and trying to figure out how to access specific rooms and what to do with the items I found. I am proud to say that I solved almost all the puzzles on my own. In order to do so, it was important to keep notes while playing. I drew up all the maps in my notebook and noted down hints about areas. In fact, the only two puzzles I couldn’t solve were caused by incomplete notes.

While I greatly enjoyed the exploration aspect, I detested the gunplay. The player simply stands static while pointing at an enemy and can then shoot - that’s it. Depending on faster enemies, you are required to dodge attacks and evade enemies but this is much easier said than done. Initially, I started the game using the tank controls of the original in which a press to the lift stick only turns you to the left direction - like a tank! - but you don’t move into that direction. I cannot fully comprehend how the game was designed for this mechanic in the first place. Less because I would have had problems with the tank controls themselves (I didn’t, thanks Grim Fandango!) but in order to run away from enemies, you need to turn around either by 180 degrees with your stick or use the quick-turn button. But that functionality doesn’t feel fast enough to dodge enemies. Later on, there are even a few stages where the player has to run between points in a short amount of time and this would definitely not have worked with tank controls. A bit of a shame, as I actually felt that by removing those tank controls I took some form of purity away from the game.

Another negative aspect I have to mention is item management, especially save points. Throughout the game, you have only eight item slots which are way too few considering all the things you find. Therefore, it is necessary to run back and forth to your loot box to deposit items. But much worse for me is the requirement of certain items to be able to save your progression. Due to the lack of save items, I played for much longer sessions than I would have liked to - I’m an adult man, let me manage my own time! Even worse, I often couldn’t save when I wanted to as I ran out of save items. This threw me into a vicious cycle where I died multiple times a good half an hour after having last saved because I couldn’t any longer. This caused me to repeat huge chunks of the game multiple times. I despise repeating the same section in video games; this aspect was a real torture and test of patience for me.

The game involves a lot of “backtracking” - first exploring one area, finding a locked door, going to a different area, finding the key for the previously locked door and turning back. It really helps driving home the point of not being able to escape this creepy mansion but I feel like this mechanic also overstayed its welcome. In fact, this could be said for the entire game to be honest. While I enjoyed it, I also feel like it lost steam in the lost few hours.

Although there have been a lot of negative points, I still think the game is worth playing - it’s a fun (and scary!) step back in time. But be aware of what you are getting into: Resident Evil HD Remaster is not a brand new game so don’t expect the world. What you can expect is a creepy mansion, dark atmosphere, cool puzzles and cheesy acting.

I would suggest new starters to start on the easy or very easy difficulty (I played on normal) and to immediately switch to the new alternative control scheme. I say so because what I enjoyed the most about this game were the puzzles and 90s atmosphere - but these points had been turned sour for me due to the many times I had to repeat sections over and over again. An easier difficulty would have remedied that.

item.title: Tier 711
item.description:
The date is 24 December 2027. Children like Tiff and Jill are wrapped up in their hermetically sealed onesies, eagerly awaiting Santa’s presents. What will he bring this year? A pony for Tiff? A magical dollhouse for Jill?

One floor further down, the adults are also wrapped up, but in protective hazmat gear. The glimmer of the TV reflects on Christie’s and Mike’s visors. “We are truly, outstandingly sorry, but under the current circumstances, we are unable to celebrate Christmas as planned. It was a difficult decision but one we believe is necessary: tomorrow, the region of London will move from tier 710 into the specific new tier 711.”

“711!” gasps Christie behind her helmet, causing her vision to steam up.

“In this specific new tier”, continues the prime minister, “children will be asked to vacate any living space they share with adults over the age of 23. For this purpose, our military forces have been working incredibly hard to construct housing solutions in the Docklands area.”

“This is horrible, Mike!”

“It is. But it’s for the best.”

“For the best? Those are our children!”

“They can kill us, honey!” Mike turns the TV off and gets up from the couch. His legs are sticking together from the polyester suit.

“That’s okay, I have lived my life already.”

“Christ, Christie, you just turned 26!”

“You might think it’s young, but this virus doesn’t.”

“It’s only for two weeks. Two weeks of isolation and then this will be over.”

“Of course it will. Of course!” says Christie with a sneer.

“This virus can’t live forever. If we restrain the hosts, it will die off.”

“This is so horrible.” Christie starts to cry. Mike is startled by this sight - he hasn’t seen his wife shed tears in more than a year, when the government announced a culling order for all dogs. Poor old Mr. Blinky.

Mike marches over to Christie and lets his glove sink on her shoulder. He can feel her bones through their combined layers of protection.

It turns out that Jill received her wish from Santa after all - both Tiff and Jill depart the next day to their new home for the upcoming weeks. “It’s a magical dollhouse, just like you wanted!” her mother has said. How exciting!

item.title: I hate difficulty settings in video games
item.description:
“Playing a video game on a lower difficulty is for losers. This is not how it was intended!”

A sentence like the above races through my mind every time I contemplate lowering the difficulty - but rarely do I follow through with it. The voice in my head is too strong. Usually, I end up trying over and over again if there is a particularly difficult section in a video game - which in the end simply leaves me getting more and more frustrated and takes all enjoyment from playing. Is this really what was intended?

To me, changing the video game difficulty away from the default is a psychological problem. I am painfully trying to create the experience the developers (supposedly) wanted me to have; in that way, I am vying for the author’s intent like high-school teachers are when discussing classic literature.

But how are choices on how difficult a game should be even made? Did the developers of the original Resident Evil have had their game tested for months on end by hundreds of players to find out how many items to hand out?

I have deep dismay for developers who don’t seem to understand that different people have different sets of skill - and different levels of frustration. A famous example of this is the game “Wolfenstein: The New Order” in which the game depicts the main character depending on the selected choice. When selecting the lowest difficulty, the main character is being shown with a baby’s bonnet and a dummy in their mouth.

A game like Wolfenstein is actively trying to stop the player from choosing this difficulty - but what would be the harm? It seems like many developers don’t understand that different people have different expectations of games: Some people want to feel like a mighty god. Some loathe repeating sections they have already seen. Some can’t spend that much time on gaming. Others may be handicapped in some way. Some are easily frustrated and angered.

Difficulty is such an essential element of game, almost the essence of gaming - yet the industry hasn’t made any major advancements in this area. Most video games still ask you at the start what difficulty to select - a choice no player can properly make as they haven’t even played a minute of the game yet! While most modern games nowadays allow for changes to the difficulty settings during a game session, there is still a big group of games which don’t allow the player to change the difficulty once they have started.

While most triple-A titles allow you to change the difficulty on the fly, they only allow you to go into one direction. Such an example is any game in the Uncharted series - you can only move from Normal to Easy, not from Easy to Normal. Other examples of this annoyance are the recent Resident Evil 2 and Resident Evil 3 remakes. The reason for only allowing the one-directional change in difficulty is usually that trophies are tied to certain difficulties. I say trophies should reward players who drain every last drop of game i.e. via meticulous exploration, not those who crave for a greater challenge.

Of course, there are a few outstanding exceptions to bland and restrictive difficulty choices, such as Resident Evil 4 which covertly readjusts the difficulty so that it’s ensured the player is always faced with a tense but fair challenge.

My mother used to say “Losers are only those who care too much about other people’s opinion”. A proverb which is absolutely right - I should lower the difficulty if I want to as it is my gaming experience. Thanks, Mom!
